title: HTTP/2 Frames, Streams, HPACK, Flow Control, and Go Boundaries · Go Semantics Case: H2 Go Transfer Boundary
summary: A team assumes HTTP/2 is compressed HTTP/1.1 text, shares one parser state across streams, ignores SETTINGS and flow control, indexes secrets into HPACK, and maps every stream failure to connection failure. Planned artifact: Add and verify the h2 go transfer boundary increment in HTTP/2 connection, stream, frame, and compression model. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Use Go 1.26 net/http and current HTTP/2 implementation for production while limiting custom work to models, frame labs, tests, and authorized disposable environments.
Coordinate stream and connection flow control, frame size, concurrent streams, backpressure, fairness, reset, GOAWAY, and retry evidence.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Predict H2 Go Transfer Boundary · Theory H2 Go Transfer Boundary
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory protocol team handles case 4903 in a partner TLS endpoint negotiating HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 through ALPN. Build deterministic Go evidence to combine HTTP/2 frame, stream, flow-control, and header-list bounds; preserve this operating constraint: diagnostics must omit credentials and attacker-controlled metric labels; then split every structural delimiter across a different read and retain the same result. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Use Go 1.26 net/http and current HTTP/2 implementation for production while limiting custom work to models, frame labs, tests, and authorized disposable environments.
